terms.def: Largest peak-to-trough decline over a period; the worst loss an investor timing the peak/trough would suffer.
CAGR / |MDD|; risk-adjusted return relative to worst historical loss. Popular in hedge fund evaluation.
Time the portfolio spends below its previous peak; long underwater periods signal slow recovery.
Time from the trough back to the previous peak; longer recovery = worse drawdown characteristics.
Total time from peak to recovery; peak-to-trough + trough-to-recovery.
Previous peak NAV; hedge fund performance fees are only charged on returns above the high-water mark.

eli3: Drawdown measures how much a portfolio has fallen from its peak value. If a portfolio reached $100 at its highest point and is now at $80, the current drawdown is 20%. Maximum drawdown (MDD) is the largest peak-to-trough decline over an entire period — it's the worst loss an investor who bought at the peak and sold at the trough would have experienced.

Drawdown matters enormously for investors because it captures something variance doesn't: the experience of watching your wealth decline. A portfolio can have low average volatility but a devastating single drawdown. Psychologically, investors typically exit at the worst time — near the trough — so drawdown is directly linked to capital destruction and investor redemptions.

Key drawdown metrics: maximum drawdown (worst peak-to-trough decline), drawdown duration (how long the portfolio was below its previous peak), Calmar ratio (annualised return / maximum drawdown), and Ulcer Index (RMS of drawdown over time). These complement Sharpe ratio by capturing the 'path' of returns, not just the average.

whenWhy.title: Computing and interpreting drawdown
whenWhy.body: Drawdown at time t: DD(t) = (V(t) - Peak(t)) / Peak(t), where Peak(t) = max_{s≤t} V(s). DD(t) ≤ 0 always (you're either at the peak or below it). Maximum drawdown: MDD = min_{t} DD(t) = max loss from any peak to any subsequent trough. Recovery time: time from trough back to previous peak. Historical MDD for major asset classes: S&P 500: -55% (2007-2009 financial crisis), -49% (2000-2002 dot-com), -34% (2020 COVID). Bond indices: typically -10 to -20% in worst rate environments. Diversified 60/40: approximately -30 to -40% in major crises. Calmar ratio: annualised_return / |MDD|. A fund earning 10%/year with MDD = -20% has Calmar = 0.5; a fund earning 10%/year with MDD = -10% has Calmar = 1.0 — significantly better from a drawdown perspective despite identical return.

whenWhy.title: Drawdown control strategies
whenWhy.body: Volatility targeting: scale portfolio weights so realised portfolio volatility stays near a target (e.g., 10%). When volatility rises, reduce exposure; when volatility falls, increase exposure. Implementation: w_t = target_vol / σ_t × w_baseline. Effect on drawdown: during market crashes (when vol spikes), positions are automatically reduced — limits drawdown at the cost of reduced participation in early recovery. Historically: volatility targeting reduces MDD by 30-50% while capturing ~70-80% of upside returns. Drawdown stop-loss: hard limit — if portfolio drawdown exceeds X%, reduce to cash or reduce risk to minimum. Risk: if triggered near the trough, you're selling into weakness and miss the recovery. Better implementation: gradual de-risking as drawdown increases, not binary on/off. CPPIs (Constant Proportion Portfolio Insurance): dynamically allocates between risky portfolio and risk-free asset based on cushion (portfolio value - floor). When cushion shrinks, risky allocation is reduced mechanically — guarantees the floor at the cost of performance gap in trending markets.

whenWhy.title: Drawdown as a fund management constraint
whenWhy.body: For hedge fund managers: drawdown is the most critical risk metric from investors' perspective. Typical hedge fund mandates: maximum drawdown limit of -15% to -20% from HWM (high water mark); if breached, fund triggers a risk reduction or winds down. High water mark (HWM): performance fees are only earned on new peaks — if a fund is below HWM, it must recover all losses before earning new fees. HWM creates an option-like incentive: at large drawdowns, managers may take more risk (to earn fees) or quit (if recovery seems impossible). 'Zombie funds': funds far below HWM with little hope of recovery; investors are trapped (unrealised losses) while managers have no incentive to recover. Institutional investors: pension funds and endowments have drawdown constraints tied to spending requirements — a 30% drawdown may force an endowment to cut university distributions, making MDD a hardcoded constraint in ALM (asset-liability management).

deepDiveIntro: The drawdown at any point in time is the percentage by which the portfolio has fallen from its all-time high up to that date. The Ulcer Index is a summary measure that penalises both how deep and how long the portfolio spends underwater, computed as the square root of the average squared drawdown over time. The Sterling ratio divides annualised return by the average of the N largest drawdowns, which is more stable than Calmar because it is not driven by a single worst event. For a portfolio following geometric Brownian motion, the expected maximum drawdown is inversely proportional to the Sharpe ratio — a strategy with a higher risk-adjusted return is expected to experience smaller drawdowns over the same period.

deepDive.heading: Drawdown Distribution Under GBM
deepDive.body: Research by Magdon-Ismail & Atiya (2004) shows that the expected maximum drawdown of a portfolio following geometric Brownian motion grows with the square root of time and is inversely related to the Sharpe ratio. For a strategy with a Sharpe ratio of 1 — considered genuinely good — a 10-year period will, on average, produce a maximum drawdown of around 35–40%. This is a sobering reminder that large drawdowns are normal even for high-quality strategies; investors who expect a Sharpe-1 strategy to stay within a 10% drawdown will almost certainly be disappointed. [1]

deepDive.heading: Drawdown vs. Volatility: Different Risk Perspectives
deepDive.body: Volatility treats upward and downward swings symmetrically and does not capture the severity of sustained declines. Drawdown measures cumulative loss from a peak, so it is sensitive to how losses are distributed over time: a portfolio that loses steadily over six months is much worse in drawdown terms than one with the same total volatility but random daily swings. Momentum strategies illustrate this: moderate daily volatility can coexist with catastrophic drawdowns when momentum crashes (the 2009 reversal produced a 73% peak-to-trough loss in three months). Risk limits based on volatility or tracking error can therefore severely underestimate the actual drawdown experience for path-dependent strategies. [2]

deepDive.heading: Underwater Equity Curves and Psychological Impact
deepDive.body: After the dot-com peak in 2000, the S&P 500 took 13 years to recover its high; after the Great Depression peak in 1929, it took 25 years. These recovery periods are far longer than most investors' stated time horizons, which means real investors are often forced to sell during the recovery phase and crystallise their losses. Loss aversion — the well-documented finding that losses feel about twice as painful as equivalent gains — makes drawdowns psychologically devastating in proportion to their magnitude. For professional managers, the agency problem compounds this: underperforming a benchmark for two years often triggers client redemptions regardless of the strategy's long-term merits, effectively shortening the investment horizon of even patient strategies. [2]

takeaways: Maximum drawdown: largest peak-to-trough decline; Calmar ratio = annual return / |MDD|. [1]
Expected MDD for Sharpe = 1 strategy over 10 years: ~35-40% — normal, not evidence of failure. [1]
Volatility targeting: scale position by target_vol / realized_vol; reduces MDD ~30-50% with modest return sacrifice. [1]
CPPI: allocates between risky and safe assets based on cushion; guarantees floor but creates performance gap. [2]
Loss aversion: drawdowns cause disproportionate investor pain; redemption risk peaks near trough. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you distinguish a temporary drawdown from a strategy breakdown?
edgeCases.a: Distinguishing a 'normal' drawdown (within expected distribution) from a structural breakdown: (1) Statistical test: compute the probability of observing the current drawdown given the strategy's historical volatility and Sharpe ratio. If P(MDD ≥ observed | strategy is valid) < 5%, investigate further; (2) Factor attribution: during the drawdown, decompose losses into factor exposures. Is the drawdown explained by known risk factors (e.g., momentum strategy losing during a market recovery — expected) or by something unexplained? (3) Peer comparison: are other strategies with similar exposures also suffering? If yes → market regime change, not strategy-specific breakdown; if no → strategy-specific issue; (4) Signal behaviour: is the strategy's alpha signal (e.g., IC) still positive during the drawdown? If IC remains positive but returns are negative → probably adverse factor returns, not signal breakdown; if IC has collapsed → possible strategy breakdown. Rule of thumb: drawdowns within 2× the historical MDD are likely 'normal'; drawdowns exceeding 2× historical MDD warrant serious investigation.

edgeCases.q: How should drawdown limits be set for different investor types?
edgeCases.a: Drawdown limits should be calibrated to the investor's actual risk tolerance and liquidity horizon, not generic: (1) Endowments/foundations (spending rate 4-5%/year): MDD limit ≈ -20 to -30%; they need to fund spending even in drawdowns, so capital preservation matters; (2) Pension funds (liability-driven): drawdown limit driven by funding ratio; a -20% equity loss might reduce funding ratio from 110% to 90% — breaching minimum funding requirements; (3) Hedge fund limited partners: typically -15 to -20% MDD limit with 3-6 month notice; beyond this, consider redemption; (4) Sovereign wealth funds (multi-generational): can tolerate MDD of -40 to -50% due to very long horizon; (5) Family offices: depends on concentration and liquidity needs; typically -25 to -35%. Key principle: drawdown limit should be set so that breaching it causes genuine harm (forced liquidation, spending cut) — not just discomfort. Setting overly tight limits creates perverse incentives for portfolio managers to take less risk than optimal.

sources.label: Magdon-Ismail, M. & Atiya, A.F. (2004) — Maximum Drawdown, Risk Magazine
Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979) — Prospect Theory, Econometrica
